hero_headline: Federal money is chasing AI, and so is the cheating debate. Here is what that means for us.
intro: Summer reading, AI edition. This month the U.S. Department of Education signaled it wants grant dollars to flow toward AI, which is worth watching as a leadership and budget question. At the same time, the honest conversation about AI and cheating is getting louder, with some schools literally bringing back blue books. We also pulled a few practical pieces on differentiated learning, lesson planning, and teacher burnout so the news is not all hand wringing.

items.title: The Ed. Dept. Wants to Steer Grant Money to AI. What That Means for Schools
items.takeaway: Federal grant priorities may start favoring AI, so it is worth understanding before the next funding cycle hits your district.

items.title: My greatest concern for AI in education: the invisible handshake
items.takeaway: A leadership take on the real risk: an illusion of learning where everyone feels satisfied but nothing of substance is actually happening.

items.title: Differentiated Learning Powered by AI
items.takeaway: Concrete ways to use AI to adapt content to reading levels and build flexible groups, so you spend more time with kids and less on prep.

items.title: Customizing Lesson Plans with AI: Meeting Diverse Student Needs
items.takeaway: A practical walkthrough for tailoring a single lesson to a range of learners without rewriting it five times by hand.

items.title: How AI Can Ease K-12 Teacher Burnout
items.takeaway: If the admin load is wearing your staff down, here are the tasks AI can quietly take off their plates.

items.title: The Influence of AI Tools on the Student Writing Process and Compositional Fluency
items.takeaway: Early research on what AI actually does to how students write, useful context before you set a classroom policy.

sections.name: Proceed With Caution
sections.note: A standing spot for the honest watch-outs. AI is a tool, not a shortcut around judgment.

items.title: AI Cheating Is So Out of Hand That the Blue Books Are Coming Back
items.takeaway: Some schools are returning to handwritten exams, a blunt reminder that assessment design has to account for AI now.

items.title: Ethical Use of ChatGPT in Education: Best Practices to Combat AI-Induced Plagiarism
items.takeaway: If you want a policy grounded in research rather than gut feeling, start here for combating AI plagiarism.

items.title: Phones Were Banned So Students Couldn't Use ChatGPT. The Problem Came From the Teachers
items.takeaway: A reminder that an AI policy aimed only at students misses half the room.

items.title: Let's Talk About ChatGPT and Cheating in the Classroom
items.takeaway: A 20 minute listen that frames the cheating debate without the panic, good for a commute.

editor_pick.title: The Ed. Dept. Wants to Steer Grant Money to AI. What That Means for Schools
editor_pick.blurb: This is the one to read if you only read one. Funding priorities shape what districts actually do, and a federal nudge toward AI is the kind of signal that turns into budget lines and grant applications down the road.
